title: Changes, Claims and Disputes
description: Learn how engineering projects manage contract changes, contractual claims and disputes, including change management, variation procedures, documentation, negotiation, dispute resolution and practical contract administration.

sections.title: Introduction
content: Engineering projects rarely proceed exactly as originally planned.
Designs evolve, client requirements change, unforeseen conditions arise and external events affect project execution.
Contracts therefore include procedures for handling changes, submitting claims and resolving disputes.
Following these procedures helps reduce conflict and keeps projects moving efficiently.
Poor contract administration often creates larger problems than the original change itself.

sections.title: Why Changes Occur
content: Projects operate in uncertain environments where not every condition can be predicted.
Changes may result from client requests, design improvements, new regulations, unforeseen site conditions or supplier issues.
Some changes improve project value, while others are necessary to address unexpected problems.
The objective is not to avoid every change but to manage them effectively.

sections.title: What Is a Contract Change?
content: A contract change modifies one or more contractual obligations.
Changes may affect scope, specifications, schedule, cost, quality or responsibilities.
Most engineering contracts define formal procedures before changes become binding.
Verbal instructions should normally be confirmed in writing.

sections.title: Scope Changes
content: Scope changes modify the work that must be performed.
Examples include additional equipment, revised layouts or new technical requirements.
Even small scope changes may affect cost, schedule and project risk.
The full impact should be evaluated before implementation.

sections.title: Variations
content: Many engineering contracts refer to contract changes as variations.
A variation is a formal modification instructed or approved under the contract.
The contract normally specifies who may issue a variation and how it should be valued.
Unauthorized changes may not create entitlement.

sections.title: Change Management
content: Change management is the structured process used to evaluate, approve and implement changes.
Good change management protects both technical performance and commercial interests.
Every significant change should be assessed before execution whenever possible.
Clear documentation is essential throughout the process.

sections.title: The Change Management Process
content: A typical process begins with identifying a proposed change.
The technical, schedule, cost and risk impacts are then evaluated.
Appropriate approval is obtained before implementation.
The contract price or completion date is adjusted if required.
Finally, project documents are updated.

sections.title: Change Requests
content: A change request formally describes a proposed modification.
It should explain the reason for the change, affected work and expected consequences.
Supporting drawings, calculations and estimates may be attached.
The request provides the basis for technical and commercial evaluation.

sections.title: Evaluating Changes
content: Changes should be evaluated from multiple perspectives.
Technical feasibility, safety, quality, procurement, schedule and commercial impacts should all be considered.
A technically beneficial change may still be commercially unattractive.
Decision-makers require complete information before approval.

sections.title: Impact on Cost
content: Changes frequently create additional labor, materials and equipment costs.
Indirect costs such as supervision or extended project duration may also arise.
Some changes reduce costs rather than increase them.
Cost estimates should identify both direct and indirect effects.

sections.title: Impact on Schedule
content: Changes often influence project completion dates.
Additional work, procurement delays or redesign may extend the schedule.
Some changes can also accelerate completion.
Schedule impacts should be analyzed using updated project planning tools.

sections.title: Impact on Risk
content: Changes may introduce new technical or commercial risks.
Existing risks may increase or decrease.
The project's risk register should be updated whenever significant changes occur.
Risk assessments support informed decision-making.

sections.title: Contractual Authority
content: Not everyone on a project has authority to change the contract.
The agreement usually specifies which representatives may approve variations.
Performing major work based on unauthorized instructions creates commercial risk.
Authority should always be verified.

sections.title: What Is a Claim?
content: A claim is a formal request for contractual relief.
The relief may involve additional payment, extra time or another contractual remedy.
Claims should follow the procedures specified in the contract.
A claim is not automatically a dispute.

sections.title: Common Causes of Claims
content: Claims commonly arise from delayed information, design changes, differing site conditions and late delivery.
Acceleration, suspension, weather events and client interference may also generate claims.
Proper documentation is essential regardless of the cause.

sections.title: Types of Claims
content: Engineering projects may involve time claims, cost claims or combined time-and-cost claims.
Other claims relate to defects, warranties, disruption or termination.
The contract determines the available remedies.

sections.title: Extension of Time Claims
content: An extension of time adjusts the contractual completion date.
The contractor must normally demonstrate that an entitled event delayed critical project activities.
Supporting schedules and records are usually required.

sections.title: Additional Cost Claims
content: A contractor may seek reimbursement for additional costs caused by events allocated to the client.
Typical examples include idle labor, equipment standby and redesign costs.
Accurate financial records strengthen the claim.

sections.title: Concurrent Delay
content: Concurrent delay occurs when both parties contribute to project delay during the same period.
These situations are often complex.
The contractual treatment varies between legal systems and contract forms.
Careful schedule analysis is usually required.

sections.title: Notice Requirements
content: Most contracts require timely notice when claim events occur.
Notice informs the other party that contractual relief may be requested.
Late notice may reduce or eliminate entitlement.
Project teams should understand all contractual deadlines.

sections.title: Supporting Documentation
content: Claims are only as strong as the supporting evidence.
Useful documents include schedules, correspondence, meeting minutes, photographs, inspection reports and cost records.
Daily site reports often become valuable evidence months later.

sections.title: Record Keeping
content: Accurate project records reduce uncertainty during claim evaluation.
Records should be created continuously rather than reconstructed later.
Incomplete documentation weakens otherwise valid claims.

sections.title: Burden of Proof
content: The claiming party normally carries the burden of proving entitlement.
It must demonstrate the contractual basis, causation and resulting impact.
Assertions without evidence rarely succeed.

sections.title: Causation
content: A successful claim requires a clear link between the event and the claimed loss.
Not every additional cost automatically results from the relevant change.
The relationship between cause and effect should be demonstrated using objective evidence.

sections.title: Mitigation
content: Both parties should take reasonable steps to reduce project impacts.
A contractor should not unnecessarily increase delay or cost.
Reasonable mitigation efforts may strengthen later claims.

sections.title: Negotiation
content: Most engineering claims are resolved through negotiation.
Negotiation is generally faster, cheaper and less disruptive than formal dispute resolution.
Successful negotiations rely on facts, documentation and realistic expectations.

sections.title: Claim Evaluation
content: The receiving party reviews entitlement, supporting evidence and contractual compliance.
Technical, commercial and legal personnel often participate in the evaluation.
Additional information may be requested before a decision is made.

sections.title: Settlement Agreements
content: Parties may resolve claims through negotiated settlement.
Settlement often involves compromise from both sides.
The agreement should clearly define what issues are fully resolved.

sections.title: What Is a Dispute?
content: A dispute exists when the parties disagree on contractual rights or obligations.
Many claims never become disputes because they are resolved through negotiation.
Disputes usually arise after negotiations fail.

sections.title: Common Causes of Disputes
content: Disputes frequently involve payment, delays, design responsibility, defects and contract interpretation.
Poor communication and inadequate documentation often make disagreements worse.

sections.title: Preventing Disputes
content: Many disputes can be avoided through early communication and good contract administration.
Promptly addressing issues before they escalate is often the most effective strategy.
Maintaining trust between project participants also reduces conflict.

sections.title: Negotiation as Dispute Resolution
content: Negotiation remains the preferred first step even after a dispute develops.
Parties maintain greater control over the outcome than in formal proceedings.
Commercial relationships are often preserved.

sections.title: Mediation
content: Mediation uses an independent third party to facilitate discussions.
The mediator does not impose a binding decision.
The parties remain responsible for reaching their own agreement.

sections.title: Adjudication
content: Some contracts provide adjudication for rapid dispute decisions during project execution.
The adjudicator reviews evidence and issues a decision within a relatively short period.
The legal effect depends on the contract and applicable law.

sections.title: Arbitration
content: Arbitration is a private dispute resolution process.
Independent arbitrators hear evidence before issuing a binding award.
International engineering contracts frequently use arbitration.

sections.title: Litigation
content: Litigation resolves disputes through the court system.
Court proceedings may be lengthy, expensive and public.
Litigation is often considered a last resort.

sections.title: Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR)
content: ADR includes negotiation, mediation and arbitration.
These methods often reduce cost and preserve commercial relationships compared with litigation.

sections.title: Dispute Resolution Clauses
content: Engineering contracts normally specify how disputes must be handled.
The clause may require negotiation before mediation or arbitration.
Following the agreed process is important.

sections.title: The Role of Documentation
content: Well-maintained documentation is often the strongest evidence in both claims and disputes.
Project correspondence should remain factual, accurate and professional.
Good documentation protects all parties.

sections.title: Project Correspondence
content: Letters, emails and meeting minutes should clearly describe project events.
Professional communication reduces misunderstandings and creates reliable records.
Emotional language should be avoided.

sections.title: Practical Scenario: Design Change
content: The client requests larger equipment after manufacturing has begun.
The contractor should evaluate technical feasibility, estimate cost and schedule impacts, obtain formal approval and document the agreed variation.

sections.title: Practical Scenario: Late Drawings
content: The contractor cannot continue work because critical drawings arrive three weeks late.
The contractor should issue notice, document the delay, mitigate where possible and prepare supporting evidence for any extension-of-time claim.

sections.title: Practical Scenario: Payment Dispute
content: The contractor submits a variation claim, but the client rejects part of the valuation.
The parties should first review the contract, supporting records and valuation methodology before escalating the disagreement.

sections.title: Common Mistakes
content: Beginning changed work without written approval.
Ignoring notice deadlines.
Keeping poor project records.
Failing to evaluate schedule impacts.
Treating every disagreement as a dispute immediately.
Allowing commercial discussions to replace contractual procedures.

sections.title: Best Practices
content: Use formal change procedures.
Document every significant event.
Issue notices on time.
Maintain detailed project records.
Communicate openly with project participants.
Attempt negotiation before formal dispute resolution.
Understand the contract before taking action.

sections.title: Role of the Project Manager
content: Project managers coordinate change management throughout the project.
They ensure changes are properly evaluated, approved and documented.
They also identify potential claims early and encourage practical resolution before disputes develop.

sections.title: Summary
content: Changes are a normal part of engineering projects.
Contracts provide structured procedures for managing changes, submitting claims and resolving disputes.
Successful contract administration depends on timely notices, thorough documentation and effective communication.
Most disputes can be reduced or resolved before reaching formal legal proceedings.

sections.title: Key Takeaways
content: Not every change becomes a claim.
Not every claim becomes a dispute.
Follow contractual procedures carefully.
Issue notices within required deadlines.
Support every claim with objective evidence.
Maintain accurate project records.
Use negotiation whenever practical.
Escalate disputes only when necessary.
Strong contract administration reduces project risk.
